body:
US Digital Asset Radar tracks the United States' digital-asset regulatory landscape at the federal level -- the statutes, agency rules, interpretive letters, orders, and staff guidance of the federal bodies with a role in this space. This page is a plain-English map to get oriented before exploring the pillar boards, the trajectory board, or the document library. Every claim on this site traces to a primary source; this page summarizes that record, not a substitute for reading the sources themselves.

A scope limitation before anything else, the U.S. counterpart to how this site treats EU member-state authorities: this radar covers the federal level only. State-level regimes -- money-transmitter licensing generally, the New York State Department of Financial Services' BitLicense specifically, state trust-company chartering, and state-qualified payment stablecoin issuer regimes beyond the federal certification mechanics connecting to them -- are real and consequential, and often the regime a given business actually operates under day to day. They are out of scope here. A reader who needs to know whether a specific firm holds a state license or charter should consult that state's regulator directly, not this site.

Who regulates what at the federal level

The United States has no single federal digital-asset regulator, and that split of authority is itself the structural story this site follows, not a fact obscured by the coverage. The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates crypto assets that are securities and the venues, intermediaries, and investment products around them: exchanges and alternative trading systems for crypto-asset securities, broker-dealer and adviser custody of those securities, tokenized-securities issuance, and exchange-traded products, most recently under the generic listing standards for Commodity-Based Trust Shares it approved on 17 September 2025. The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) regulates derivatives markets in commodity-classified crypto assets -- designated contract markets and swap execution facilities under the Commodity Exchange Act -- and has long acknowledged that current law does not give it direct, comprehensive authority over spot markets in those same assets absent new legislation. Where the two agencies' remits meet, they have published joint material describing how they read the boundary: a September 2025 staff joint statement on spot crypto-asset products traded on registered exchanges, and a March 2026 joint interpretation -- an SEC release with accompanying CFTC guidance -- setting out a five-category taxonomy, presented here as what the agencies themselves published, not a resolved dispute or a win for either side. The Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN), a Treasury bureau, regulates crypto businesses as money services businesses under the Bank Secrecy Act, requiring registration, an anti-money-laundering program, and travel-rule reporting; the Office of Foreign Assets Control, also at Treasury, administers sanctions compliance alongside FinCEN's regime. The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) charters and supervises national banks and national trust banks, including several digital-asset-native charters since December 2025, and has stated through interpretive letters that national banks may provide crypto-asset custody and related stablecoin activities. The Federal Reserve supervises bank holding companies and state member banks, holds payment-system responsibilities, and -- under the GENIUS Act -- is a primary federal regulator for stablecoin issuers organized as state-member-bank subsidiaries.

What is already in force, and what is not yet

The clearest already-in-force-versus-not-yet distinction here concerns the GENIUS Act (Public Law 119-27), enacted 18 July 2025. The Act itself is enacted federal law: it establishes a permitted-issuer framework for payment stablecoins, with reserve, disclosure, and interest-prohibition requirements written into the statute. But the regime it creates is not yet effective. Its effective-date formula is the earlier of 18 months after enactment (18 January 2027) or 120 days after the primary federal payment stablecoin regulators issue final implementing regulations -- and as of this writing only proposed rules, not final ones, have issued, so only the first branch currently gives a fixed date. Implementing rulemaking is in progress across the OCC, FinCEN and OFAC jointly, the FDIC, Treasury, and the Federal Reserve, each at the proposed-rule stage; this site treats the statute's text as enacted law and each agency's proposal as exactly that -- not yet binding. Several other federal frameworks that predate or sit alongside the GENIUS Act are already in force without qualification: FinCEN's money-transmitter treatment of crypto businesses and the crypto travel rule, the OCC's custody and stablecoin-related interpretive letters, and the SEC's generic listing standards for crypto exchange-traded products.

What is genuinely pending

Federal market-structure legislation that would allocate spot-market jurisdiction between the SEC and CFTC -- the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act -- has passed the House and been reported by the Senate Banking Committee, but is not enacted law; this site records only its official procedural status, not its substance, until it becomes law. Several SEC rulemaking items on crypto market structure, crypto-asset offerings, and broker-dealer financial-responsibility rules sit on the SEC's own regulatory agenda at the proposed-rule stage; an agenda entry states an agency's planned next step, not a rule already in effect, and this site treats it accordingly.

How to use this site

The pillar boards give the current federal-level state of each of the site's seven regulatory areas in one paragraph each. The trajectory board lists officially announced or statutorily fixed upcoming dates in one place. The document library indexes every primary-source document the watcher has observed. The glossary defines the jargon used throughout, and terms shared with other jurisdictions link to a single cross-jurisdiction entry. Every summary card cites the specific primary source it is drawn from.

AI-generated summary for general information. Not legal or regulatory advice. Always verify against the linked primary source.
